Data Collection

Introduction

Collect enough and meaningful data for following steps

Goals

Collect the match data of Messi and his major honors (such as the Ballon d’Or, league championships, Champions League titles, etc.) and his player profile.

Motivation

This project uses public and legal data about Lionel Messi’s matches. The data include goals, assists, appearances, match events, and major honors, all from reliable sources. The dataset is detailed enough to support analysis of performance trends, contributions, and statistical modeling.

Data Source Information

The data used in this project is collected from Wikipedia and FootyStats.

Data Structure and Format

This project includes five CSV files and one JSON file:

  • messi_detailed.csv
    Contains detailed match information such as yellow cards, playing time, and goal involvement efficiency, sourced from FootyStats.

  • messi_club_career.csv
    Retrieved via API from Wikipedia, providing brief data on Messi’s club career.

  • messi_international_career.csv
    Retrieved via API from Wikipedia, providing brief data on Messi’s national team career.

  • honor.csv
    Manually compiled, recording Messi’s team and individual honors.

  • position.csv
    Manually compiled, recording appearances, goals, and assists by position.

  • Lionel_Messi_Player_Profile.json
    Retrieved via API from Wikipedia, containing data on Messi’s playing style.

Definition of Modeling Targets

I define several supervised learning targets to analyze and predict different aspects of Messi’s seasonal performance and ranking outcomes.

1. Regression Targets

  • Seasonal goals scored
  • Seasonal assists provided

2. Multi-output Regression Targets

  • Player rankings in a season:
    • League ranking among top attackers
    • League ranking among top midfielders
    • League ranking among top defenders
    • Club ranking as top scorer
  • Attack level score: Level 1 (low) to Level 5 (high)

3. Binary Classification Targets

  • Yellow card occurrence: 0 = No yellow card, 1 = At least one yellow card
  • Penalty occurrence: 0 = No penalty, 1 = At least one penalty

4. Multiclass Classification Target

  • Player attacking level classification (Low, Medium, High)

Overview of Methods

  • I used web scraping to collect Messi’s data from Wikipedia

  • I also extracted the “Player profile” section to study Messi’s playing style and technical characteristics.

  • For Messi’s honors, because scraping was difficult, I manually compiled the data into a CSV file with the professor’s permission.

Tools and Technologies

  • Python: Main analytical language
  • Pandas: Data cleaning and processing
  • Scikit-learn: Clustering and regression models
  • Matplotlib / Seaborn: Visualization of analysis results

Code

The code used in this section is adapted from a research1 and has been modified to focus specifically on Lionel Messi’s club and international data. The script retrieves and parses HTML content from Wikipedia pages using the requests and BeautifulSoup libraries, and then uses pandas.read_html() to convert the tables into structured DataFrames, providing a foundation for subsequent analysis.

Data of Lionel Messi’s Club Career or international Career

# import necessary libraries
import requests
import pandas as pd
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
import json
# URL of the Wikipedia page 
url = "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lionel_Messi"

# HTTP headers to mimic a browser request
headers = {
    "User-Agent": "Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) "
                  "AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) "
                  "Chrome/120.0.0.0 Safari/537.36"
}

# Send the GET request
response = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
response.raise_for_status()

# Parse the HTML content
soup = BeautifulSoup(response.content, "lxml")

# Find all tables with class "wikitable"
tables = soup.find_all("table", {"class": "wikitable"})

# Locate the "Club career statistics" table
club_table = None
for table in tables:
    headers = [th.get_text(strip=True) for th in table.find_all("th")]
    # Club career table usually has columns like "Season", "Club", "League", "Apps", "Goals"
    if any("Season" in h or "Club" in h for h in headers):
        club_table = table
        break

# Raise an exception if the table was not found
if club_table is None:
    raise Exception("Club career table not found")

# Use pandas to read the HTML table into a DataFrame
df = pd.read_html(str(club_table))[0]

# Display column names
print(df.columns)

# Save the DataFrame as a CSV file
# df.to_csv("/Users/hanmingyang/Desktop/lionel_messi_club_career.csv", index=False, encoding="utf-8-sig")
# print("CSV file has been saved as lionel_messi_club_career.csv")
MultiIndex([(           'Club',     'Club'),
            (         'Season',   'Season'),
            (         'League', 'Division'),
            (         'League',     'Apps'),
            (         'League',    'Goals'),
            ('National cup[a]',     'Apps'),
            ('National cup[a]',    'Goals'),
            ( 'Continental[b]',     'Apps'),
            ( 'Continental[b]',    'Goals'),
            (          'Other',     'Apps'),
            (          'Other',    'Goals'),
            (          'Total',     'Apps'),
            (          'Total',    'Goals')],
           )
/var/folders/fd/zfxd46k90qd0bd_xk04w45r40000gn/T/ipykernel_80341/984477767.py:35: FutureWarning: Passing literal html to 'read_html' is deprecated and will be removed in a future version. To read from a literal string, wrap it in a 'StringIO' object.
  df = pd.read_html(str(club_table))[0]
df.dtypes
Club             Club        object
Season           Season      object
League           Division    object
                 Apps         int64
                 Goals        int64
National cup[a]  Apps        object
                 Goals       object
Continental[b]   Apps        object
                 Goals       object
Other            Apps        object
                 Goals       object
Total            Apps         int64
                 Goals        int64
dtype: object
df.head()

Club Season Division Apps Goals Apps Goals Apps Goals Apps Goals Apps Goals
0 Barcelona C 2003–04[422] Tercera División 10 5 — — — — — — 10 5
1 Barcelona B 2003–04[423] Segunda División B 5 0 — — — — — — 5 0
2 Barcelona B 2004–05[424] Segunda División B 17 6 — — — — — — 17 6

Data of Lionel Messi’s Player profile


# Parse the HTML content
soup = BeautifulSoup(response.text, "html.parser")

# Find the "Player profile" section header
player_profile_header = soup.find(id="Player_profile")
if not player_profile_header:
    raise ValueError("Player profile section not found")

# Initialize data dictionary
data = {
    "section": "Player profile",
    "paragraphs": []
}

# Iterate over the sibling elements after the header, capturing only the first 7 paragraphs
paragraph_count = 0
for sibling in player_profile_header.parent.find_next_siblings():
    if sibling.name == "h2":
        break
    if sibling.name == "p":
        text = sibling.get_text(strip=True)
        if text:
            data["paragraphs"].append(text)
            paragraph_count += 1
            if paragraph_count >= 7:  # Limit the number of paragraphs
                break

# Save the data as a JSON file
# with open("/Users/hanmingyang/Desktop/Lionel_Messi_Player_Profile.json", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    # json.dump(data, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=4)

# print("Saved as Lionel_Messi_Player_Profile.json")

The initial extracted data contained issues such as abnormal labels and inconsistent formats. These problems will be addressed in the following part.

Summary

Challenges

I met some challenges. During the crawling, i found that some pages (such as the section about Messi’s honors) had complex structures and difficult to capture. Professor James suggested that i can use the large language models to extract data. However, there were still some data that could not be capture. After getting the professor’s permission, I manually organized the textual data and compiled it into CSV files.

Future Work

The next steps I will focus on data cleaning for following analysis

References

1.
Abodayeh, A., Hejazi, R., Najjar, W., Shihadeh, L. & Latif, R. Web scraping for data analytics: A BeautifulSoup implementation. 2023 Sixth International Conference of Women in Data Science at Prince Sultan University (WiDS PSU) 65–69 (2023).
